task: I’m getting my tires rotated soon and want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything basic. Can you help me figure out what I should check before I drive off, and whether there’s anything specific I should ask them to verify while it’s on the lift?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a practical tire-rotation check that covers the basics without being fussy.

## Before you drive off
Do a quick walkaround and confirm:

- **Lug nuts are installed and seated**
  - No missing lugs, no obvious cross-threading, no wheel sitting crooked.
- **Tire pressures are correct**
  - Ask them to set all four to the **door-jamb placard spec** unless you want a different pressure.
- **Wheel caps / center caps are back on**
  - If your wheels use them, make sure none are missing or loose.
- **No warning lights**
  - Especially TPMS, ABS, or anything new that wasn’t on before.
- **No obvious rubbing or noise**
  - Turn the steering wheel lock-to-lock if you can, and make sure nothing sounds off.
- **Short test drive feel**
  - The car should track straight and feel normal at highway speed, with no vibration.

## Ask them to verify while it’s on the lift
These are the useful “since it’s already up there” checks:

- **Tire wear pattern**
  - Ask if they see any uneven wear that suggests alignment, inflation, or suspension issues.
- **Tread depth**
  - Especially if one tire is wearing faster than the others.
- **Sidewall damage / bulges / embedded objects**
  - Good time to catch a problem before it becomes a roadside issue.
- **Brake condition**
  - Quick look at pad thickness and rotor condition is reasonable.
- **Suspension / steering play**
  - Ask them to note anything loose in ball joints, tie rods, or bushings.
- **Leaks**
  - Any oil, coolant, brake fluid, or CV grease around the corners/wheels.
- **TPMS sensor condition**
  - If they have the wheels off, they can often spot damaged stems or sensor issues.

## Good questions to ask the shop
You can keep it simple:

- “Can you torque the lug nuts to spec and tell me the torque value?”
- “Can you set tire pressures to the door sticker?”
- “Do you see any uneven wear or anything that suggests I need an alignment?”
- “Any signs of brake, suspension, or leak issues while it’s up?”

## One thing people often forget
If the tires were rotated to a different position, ask whether they want you to **recheck lug torque after 50–100 miles**. Many shops do, many don’t — but it’s a good habit if you’re the cautious type.

If you want, I can turn this into a **very short pre-drive checklist** you can keep on your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly good. A couple things I’d want spelled out a little more before I leave:

- **Confirm the rotation pattern they used**. I want to know if they actually rotated front-to-rear, cross-rotated, or left them as-is for directional tires. If the tires are directional or staggered, that changes what’s even possible.
- **Ask them to check torque after the test drive, if they do one**. Not just “torqued to spec,” but that they used a torque wrench, not an impact gun and a prayer.
- **Verify TPMS relearn if needed**. Some cars need it after rotation, some don’t, but if the light comes on later I want to know whether they handled that or if I need to.
- **Look at the date codes / tread depth if one tire is suspect**. I’ve had a shop miss a wrong-spec tire before, so I’m a little picky on anything that looks off.

Also, on the lift I’d specifically ask them to check:
- inner tread wear
- cupping/scalloping
- any bent wheel or damage around the bead area
- brake hose condition near each corner

What you gave is fine as a general checklist, but I’d want those extra specifics so I’m not guessing later.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter version with the specifics you want.

## Before you leave
Ask them to confirm:

- **What rotation pattern they used**
  - Front-to-rear, cross-rotation, or **no rotation** if tires are directional/staggered.
  - If the tires are **directional**, make sure they only moved side-to-side if that’s allowed, or left them as-is if not.
  - If the setup is **staggered**, confirm they understand that true rotation may not be possible.

- **Torque procedure**
  - “Did you install the wheels with a **torque wrench to spec**?”
  - If they do a test drive, ask: **“Will you recheck torque after the test drive?”**
  - You want a clear yes/no, not just “they’re tight.”

- **TPMS relearn**
  - Ask whether your car needs a **TPMS relearn/reset** after rotation.
  - If it does, ask whether they performed it or whether you need to do it.
  - Confirm whether any TPMS light is expected or not.

- **Tire condition details**
  - Ask them to note:
    - **tread depth**
    - **date codes** if one tire looks old or suspect
    - whether any tire looks mismatched in size/spec
  - If one tire looked questionable before, have them compare it to the others and tell you if anything is off.

## While it’s on the lift, ask them to check:
- **Inner tread wear**
- **Cupping / scalloping**
- **Bent wheel**
- **Bead area damage**
- **Brake hose condition at each corner**
- Also worth adding:
  - **sidewall bulges / cuts**
  - **suspension play**
  - **leaks near the corners**

## Good wording to use
You can say:

> “Can you tell me the exact rotation pattern you used, confirm torque was done with a torque wrench to spec, and let me know whether this car needs a TPMS relearn? Also, please check tread depth/date codes if anything looks suspicious, and inspect inner wear, cupping, bent wheels, bead area, and brake hoses while it’s on the lift.”

## Quick leave-with confidence check
Before pulling out:
- no warning lights
- no vibration
- steering wheel centered
- no rubbing/noise
- pressures set correctly
- caps on
- lug nuts visually seated

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-screen phone checklist** with just the exact questions to ask the shop.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the version I wanted.

That covers the rotation pattern, torque method, TPMS relearn, and the extra inspection points I care about. The wording is usable too — not fluffy.

I’d probably keep the one-screen checklist version too, honestly.
